title: Rational functions and their graphs
summary: This lesson builds the full toolkit for the rational functions on the AA SL syllabus: the reciprocal function $y=\frac{1}{x}$ and its symmetry, and the linear rational function $y=\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$. You will learn to locate the vertical asymptote (where the denominator is zero) and the horizontal asymptote (from the ratio of the leading coefficients), find the x- and y-intercepts, state the domain and range, and use all of this to produce a clean Paper 1 sketch by hand. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards marks, because on Paper 1 how you show your working decides how many marks you keep.
learningObjectives: Describe the reciprocal function $y=\frac{1}{x}$ and its key features: asymptotes, symmetry, domain and range.
Determine the vertical asymptote of $y=\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$ by setting the denominator equal to zero.
Determine the horizontal asymptote from the ratio of the leading coefficients.
Find the x- and y-intercepts and use the asymptotes and intercepts to sketch the graph.
State the domain and range of a linear rational function, and present working so that method marks and follow-through protect the final answer.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Graphing the Gaps
simpleExplanation.summary: A rational function is a fraction with a polynomial on the top and a polynomial on the bottom. Its graph often has 'walls' and 'floors' called asymptotes — lines the curve rushes towards but never actually reaches.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine sailing a ship as close as you can to a long, straight coastline. The path of your ship is the graph and the coastline is the asymptote. You can sail parallel to it for miles, getting closer and closer, but you never quite land on it.
steps: Find the vertical wall: set the denominator equal to zero and solve. That $x$-value is the vertical asymptote — and the one value missing from the domain.
Find the horizontal floor: for $y=\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$ the top and bottom have the same degree, so the horizontal asymptote is $y=\frac{a}{c}$, the ratio of the leading coefficients.
Pinpoint the crossings: the y-intercept is $f(0)$; the x-intercept comes from setting the numerator equal to zero.
Sketch: draw the asymptotes as dashed lines, plot the intercepts, then draw the two smooth branches of the hyperbola in the regions the asymptotes create.

flashcards.back: A hyperbola with a vertical asymptote $x=0$ and a horizontal asymptote $y=0$. It is undefined at $x=0$, has no intercepts, and has two branches: one in the first quadrant, one in the third. It has rotational symmetry of order 2 about the origin (it is an odd function).
Domain: $x \in \mathbb{R}, x \neq 0$. Range: $y \in \mathbb{R}, y \neq 0$. The excluded $x$-value is the vertical asymptote; the excluded $y$-value is the horizontal asymptote.
A function of the form $f(x)=\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$ with $c \neq 0$. Its graph is a hyperbola with one vertical and one horizontal asymptote — a translated, scaled version of $y=\frac{1}{x}$.
Set the denominator to zero: $cx+d=0 \Rightarrow x=-\frac{d}{c}$. The function is undefined there, so this line is the vertical asymptote and the value excluded from the domain.
Numerator and denominator have the same degree (1), so the horizontal asymptote is the ratio of the leading coefficients: $y=\frac{a}{c}$. This value is excluded from the range.
Evaluate $f(0)$. For $f(x)=\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$ this is $\frac{b}{d}$ (provided $d \neq 0$). The y-intercept is the point $\left(0,\frac{b}{d}\right)$.
A fraction is zero only when its numerator is zero (and the denominator is not). Set the numerator equal to zero: $ax+b=0 \Rightarrow x=-\frac{b}{a}$.
All real numbers except the value that makes the denominator zero: $x \in \mathbb{R}, x \neq -\frac{d}{c}$. The excluded value is exactly the vertical asymptote.
All real numbers except the horizontal asymptote value: $y \in \mathbb{R}, y \neq \frac{a}{c}$. The curve approaches this line but never takes that value.

flashcards.front: Can a graph cross its horizontal asymptote?
flashcards.back: For a linear rational function, no — but in general a horizontal asymptote only describes end behaviour as $x \to \pm\infty$, so a more complicated graph may cross it for 'middle' values of $x$. A graph can NEVER cross a vertical asymptote.

flashcards.front: Exam Trap: which polynomial gives which asymptote
flashcards.back: Vertical asymptote comes from the DENOMINATOR (set it to zero). Horizontal asymptote comes from the LEADING COEFFICIENTS (their ratio). Swapping these two is the single most common error on this topic.

quickCheck.prompt: For $f(x)=\frac{3x+1}{x-4}$, the equation of the vertical asymptote is:
For $f(x)=\frac{2x-1}{x+3}$, the equation of the horizontal asymptote is:
The y-intercept of $f(x)=\frac{2x-1}{x+3}$ is found by evaluating $f(0)$. It is:
The x-intercept of $f(x)=\frac{2x-1}{x+3}$ comes from setting the numerator to zero. It is:

faq.q: How do I remember which asymptote comes from where?
faq.a: Vertical asymptotes are about where the function BREAKS — that happens when you divide by zero, so they come from the denominator. Set the denominator equal to zero and solve. Horizontal asymptotes are about the LONG-RUN value — for very large $x$ only the leading terms matter, so they come from the ratio of the leading coefficients. Denominator to zero for vertical; leading-coefficient ratio for horizontal.

faq.q: Why is the linear rational function just a transformed $y=\frac{1}{x}$?
faq.a: Every $\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$ can be rewritten by dividing out as $\frac{a}{c}+\frac{k}{cx+d}$ for some constant $k$. That is the reciprocal shape $\frac{1}{x}$ stretched and shifted so its vertical asymptote moves to $x=-\frac{d}{c}$ and its horizontal asymptote moves to $y=\frac{a}{c}$. That is why the graph is always a hyperbola with the same two-branch shape.

faq.q: What is the difference between an asymptote and a 'hole'?
faq.a: If a factor cancels between numerator and denominator — for example $\frac{x^2-1}{x-1}=\frac{(x-1)(x+1)}{x-1}=x+1$ for $x \neq 1$ — the graph is the line $y=x+1$ with a single point missing (a hole) at $x=1$, not an asymptote. An asymptote occurs where the denominator is zero and the factor does NOT cancel, so the function genuinely blows up there.

The word comes from 'ratio'. Just as a rational number is a ratio of two integers like $\frac{3}{4}$, a rational function is a ratio of two polynomials like $\frac{x+2}{x-5}$. The whole behaviour of the graph flows from that fraction structure.

sections.content: Welcome to rational functions. The name comes from 'ratio': these functions are the quotient of two polynomials. On AA SL the two you must know completely are the reciprocal function $y=\frac{1}{x}$ and the linear rational function $y=\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$. Their graphs are hyperbolas, defined by two invisible guide lines — the asymptotes — and a couple of intercepts. Because Paper 1 is non-calculator, you have to find these features by hand and sketch from them. As in every calculation topic, the worked examples below are written exactly the way our engine marks your own practice: the method is on the page, mark by mark.
The reciprocal function $y=\frac{1}{x}$

Everything on this topic grows from one graph: the reciprocal function $y=\frac{1}{x}$. Because you can never divide by zero, the function is undefined at $x=0$, so the y-axis $x=0$ is a vertical asymptote. As $x$ grows very large the fraction $\frac{1}{x}$ shrinks towards zero, so the x-axis $y=0$ is a horizontal asymptote. The graph has two branches — one in the first quadrant where both $x$ and $y$ are positive, and one in the third quadrant where both are negative — and it has rotational symmetry of order 2 about the origin (it is an odd function). It has no intercepts, because it never reaches either axis.

items: **Symmetry:** rotational symmetry of order 2 about the origin; no x- or y-intercepts.

sections.question: The function $h$ is defined by $h(x)=\frac{1}{x}$. State (a) the equations of the two asymptotes, (b) the domain and range, and (c) explain why the graph has no axis intercepts. [4]
sections.solution:
**(a) Asymptotes.** The denominator is $x$, which is zero at $x=0$, so the vertical asymptote is $x=0$.  [A1]
As $x \to \pm\infty$, $\frac{1}{x} \to 0$, so the horizontal asymptote is $y=0$.  [A1]

**(b) Domain and range.** The only value that breaks the function is $x=0$, so the domain is $x \in \mathbb{R}, x \neq 0$.  [A1]
The curve approaches but never equals $y=0$, so the range is $y \in \mathbb{R}, y \neq 0$.  [A1]

**(c) Intercepts.** For a y-intercept we would need $h(0)$, but $x=0$ is not in the domain, so there is no y-intercept. For an x-intercept we would need the numerator to be zero, but the numerator is the constant $1$, which is never zero, so there is no x-intercept.

sections.content: The linear rational function $y=\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$

A linear rational function has a linear (degree-1) polynomial on the top and on the bottom: $f(x)=\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$ with $c \neq 0$. Its graph is a hyperbola — the reciprocal shape stretched and shifted. To analyse any such function you locate four things in a fixed order: the vertical asymptote, the horizontal asymptote, the y-intercept and the x-intercept. Get those, and the sketch draws itself.

\text{Vertical asymptote: } cx+d=0 \;\Rightarrow\; x=-\tfrac{d}{c}\\
\text{Horizontal asymptote: } y=\tfrac{a}{c} \text{ (ratio of leading coefficients)}\\
\text{y-intercept: } f(0)=\tfrac{b}{d}\\
\text{x-intercept: } ax+b=0 \;\Rightarrow\; x=-\tfrac{b}{a}

Two of these features are exactly the excluded values of the domain and range. The vertical asymptote $x=-\frac{d}{c}$ is the one value the domain cannot include, and the horizontal asymptote $y=\frac{a}{c}$ is the one value the range cannot include. So once you have the asymptotes, the domain and range come for free.
Vertical asymptotes: the forbidden zones

A vertical asymptote is a vertical line the graph approaches but never crosses. It occurs where the function is undefined — for a rational function, where the denominator is zero. Think of it as a wall the curve cannot pass through. To find it, set the denominator equal to zero and solve for $x$.

The vertical asymptote always comes from the DENOMINATOR. A common slip is to set the numerator to zero instead — but the numerator gives the x-intercept, not the asymptote. Denominator zero for the wall; numerator zero for the crossing.
Horizontal asymptotes: the long-run behaviour

A horizontal asymptote tells you what $y$-value the function settles towards as $x$ becomes very large or very small ($x \to \pm\infty$). For a linear rational function the numerator and denominator both have degree 1, so for large $x$ only the leading terms $ax$ and $cx$ matter, and their ratio $\frac{ax}{cx}=\frac{a}{c}$ is the horizontal asymptote $y=\frac{a}{c}$. The general rule for comparing degrees is worth knowing because it appears in later work.

items: Let $f(x)=\frac{a_n x^n + \cdots}{b_m x^m + \cdots}$ where $n$ is the degree of the numerator and $m$ the degree of the denominator.
**Case $n < m$:** the horizontal asymptote is $y=0$ (the x-axis).
**Case $n = m$:** the horizontal asymptote is $y=\frac{a_n}{b_m}$, the ratio of the leading coefficients. This is the case for every $\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$.
**Case $n > m$:** there is no horizontal asymptote; the function grows without bound as $x \to \pm\infty$.

sections.content: Putting it all together: sketching the graph

Sketching a rational function is systematic. The asymptotes form a 'scaffold' — two dashed guide lines — and the intercepts pin the curve to the axes. Draw the asymptotes first, plot the intercepts, then draw the two branches of the hyperbola so that each branch hugs both asymptotes. If you are unsure which regions the branches sit in, test one convenient point in each region.

sections.question:
Consider $f(x)=\frac{2x+4}{x-3}$.

(a) State the equation of the vertical asymptote.
(b) State the equation of the horizontal asymptote.
(c) Find the coordinates of the y-intercept and x-intercept.
(d) State the domain and range.
(e) Hence sketch $y=f(x)$, clearly showing all asymptotes and intercepts. [8]
sections.solution:
**(a) Vertical asymptote.** Set the denominator to zero: $x-3=0 \Rightarrow x=3$.  [M1][A1]
The vertical asymptote is $x=3$.

**(b) Horizontal asymptote.** Numerator and denominator both have degree 1, so take the ratio of leading coefficients: $\frac{2}{1}=2$.  [A1]
The horizontal asymptote is $y=2$.

**(c) Intercepts.** y-intercept: $f(0)=\frac{2(0)+4}{0-3}=\frac{4}{-3}=-\frac{4}{3}$, giving $\left(0,-\frac{4}{3}\right)$.  [A1]
x-intercept: set the numerator to zero, $2x+4=0 \Rightarrow x=-2$, giving $(-2,0)$.  [A1]

**(d) Domain and range.** Domain: $x \in \mathbb{R}, x \neq 3$ (the vertical asymptote). Range: $y \in \mathbb{R}, y \neq 2$ (the horizontal asymptote).  [A1]

**(e) Sketch.** Draw dashed lines $x=3$ and $y=2$ and label them; plot $\left(0,-\frac{4}{3}\right)$ and $(-2,0)$; draw the two branches of the hyperbola approaching both asymptotes, one branch through the two plotted intercepts in the lower-left region and the other in the upper-right region.  [A1 correct shape and asymptotic behaviour]

sections.content: On Paper 1 always draw asymptotes as dashed lines and label them with their equations (for example $x=3$, $y=2$) — that labelling is often a mark in its own right. Make sure each branch gets closer and closer to the asymptotes rather than curling away from them.
Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Taking the vertical asymptote from the numerator** — it comes from the DENOMINATOR. Set $cx+d=0$, not $ax+b=0$. Setting the numerator to zero gives the x-intercept instead.
**Taking the horizontal asymptote from the constant terms** — for $\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$ it is the ratio of the LEADING coefficients $y=\frac{a}{c}$, not $\frac{b}{d}$ (that is the y-intercept).
**Confusing the x- and y-intercepts** — the y-intercept is $f(0)$; the x-intercept comes from setting the numerator to zero. Swapping them is a frequent slip.
**Forgetting to exclude the vertical asymptote from the domain** — the domain is $x \in \mathbb{R}, x \neq -\frac{d}{c}$; writing 'all real numbers' loses the mark.
**Forgetting to exclude the horizontal asymptote from the range** — the range is $y \in \mathbb{R}, y \neq \frac{a}{c}$.
**Drawing the curve crossing a vertical asymptote, or leaving the asymptotes unlabelled** — a graph can never cross a vertical asymptote, and unlabelled asymptotes forfeit the labelling mark.
**Letting a branch bend away from an asymptote** — both branches must hug the guide lines as $x \to \pm\infty$.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

On Paper 1 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working — a method mark (M) or an accuracy mark (A). Follow-through (FT) means a correct step performed on your own earlier (possibly wrong) value still earns its mark, and ISW ('ignore subsequent working') means once you have written a correct answer, a clumsy later step will not be penalised again. Equivalent forms are accepted — $-\frac{1}{3}$ and $-0.333\ldots$ score the same. But that protection only exists if the method is written down. Study how each mark below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: For $f(x)=\frac{2x-1}{x+3}$, find the equations of the vertical and horizontal asymptotes and the coordinates of the axis intercepts. [6]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

**Vertical asymptote — set the denominator to zero.**
$x+3=0 \Rightarrow x=-3$.
So the vertical asymptote is $x=-3$.

**Horizontal asymptote — ratio of leading coefficients.**
Numerator and denominator are both degree 1, with leading coefficients $2$ and $1$, so
$y=\frac{2}{1}=2$.
So the horizontal asymptote is $y=2$.

**y-intercept — evaluate $f(0)$.**
$f(0)=\frac{2(0)-1}{0+3}=\frac{-1}{3}=-\frac{1}{3}$.
The y-intercept is $\left(0,-\frac{1}{3}\right)$.

**x-intercept — set the numerator to zero.**
$2x-1=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2}$.
The x-intercept is $\left(\frac{1}{2},0\right)$.

**Final answer:** vertical asymptote $x=-3$; horizontal asymptote $y=2$; y-intercept $\left(0,-\frac{1}{3}\right)$; x-intercept $\left(\frac{1}{2},0\right)$.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 6 marks:**

- **M1 — set the denominator equal to zero.** Awarded for writing $x+3=0$ (the correct METHOD for a vertical asymptote), even if the solving then slips. This is the method mark: it rewards the right approach, not the final number.
- **A1 — $x=-3$.** Awarded for the correct vertical asymptote. Accept it written as 'VA: $x=-3$'.
- **M1 — use the ratio of the leading coefficients.** Awarded for identifying $\frac{2}{1}$ (or clearly stating that equal degrees means you take the leading-coefficient ratio). Again a METHOD mark, independent of the arithmetic.
- **A1 — $y=2$.** Awarded for the correct horizontal asymptote.
- **A1 — y-intercept $\left(0,-\frac{1}{3}\right)$.** Awarded for $f(0)=-\frac{1}{3}$. Equivalent forms accepted: $-\frac{1}{3}$, $-0.333$ (3 s.f.), or the coordinate pair.
- **A1 — x-intercept $\left(\frac{1}{2},0\right)$.** Awarded for solving the numerator, $2x-1=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{2}$. Accept $0.5$.

**Follow-through (FT).** If a student mis-solved the denominator as $x=3$ but then correctly stated the domain as $x \neq 3$, or fed their own asymptote into a sketch consistently, the dependent marks are awarded on their figure. A correct process on a wrong earlier value still scores.

**Bottom line:** four of the six marks hang on writing the two methods explicitly — 'set denominator $=0$' and 'ratio of leading coefficients'. A student who writes only the four final answers with no method risks the M-marks if a single value is wrong; a student who shows each method line keeps those marks regardless.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with follow-through and equivalent forms accepted. So always show the method: write 'denominator $=0$' for the vertical asymptote, 'ratio of leading coefficients' for the horizontal asymptote, then $f(0)$ and 'numerator $=0$' for the intercepts. The method marks and follow-through are what protect your score when a number slips — and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
Where this leads

The reciprocal and linear rational functions are your first meeting with asymptotic behaviour, and the habits transfer directly. Recognising $\frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$ as a shifted, scaled $\frac{1}{x}$ is a transformations-of-graphs skill; excluding the asymptote values sharpens your command of domain and range; and setting a denominator to zero to spot where a function breaks reappears throughout calculus, in limits and in curve sketching. Master the fixed routine — denominator to zero for the vertical asymptote, leading-coefficient ratio for the horizontal, $f(0)$ and numerator-zero for the intercepts, then sketch — and every rational-function question becomes a variation on a method you already own.

sections.label: Get a Paper 1 sketch marked: find the asymptotes and intercepts of a rational function with full working

items.label: Paul's Online Math Notes: Graphing Rational Functions

subtopics.title: Rational functions of the form $f(x) = \frac{ax+b}{cx+d}$ and their graphs.
subtopics.detail: Understanding the general shape and properties of these functions.

subtopics.title: Vertical and horizontal asymptotes.
subtopics.detail: Finding equations of asymptotes from the function's formula, $x = -\frac{d}{c}$ and $y = \frac{a}{c}$.

subtopics.title: Axes intercepts.
subtopics.detail: Finding the coordinates where the graph crosses the x-axis and y-axis.

questionBank.prompt:
Consider the function $f(x) = \frac{3x-9}{x+2}$.
(a) Find the equation of the vertical asymptote. [1]
(b) Find the equation of the horizontal asymptote. [1]
(c) Find the coordinates of the point where the graph intersects the y-axis. [2]
questionBank.modelAnswer:
(a) The vertical asymptote occurs when the denominator is zero.
$x+2=0$
So the equation is $x=-2$.

(b) The horizontal asymptote is given by the ratio of the coefficients of $x$ in the numerator and denominator.
$y = \frac{3}{1}$
So the equation is $y=3$.

(c) The y-intercept occurs when $x=0$.
$f(0) = \frac{3(0)-9}{0+2} = \frac{-9}{2}$.
The coordinates are $(0, -4.5)$.

questionBank.prompt:
Let $g(x) = \frac{3x+5}{x-2}$, for $x \in \mathbb{R}, x \neq 2$.
(a) Sketch the graph of $y=g(x)$, clearly indicating any asymptotes and axes intercepts. [4]
(b) Find an expression for $g^{-1}(x)$. [2]

markScheme.text: (a) (A1) Correct vertical asymptote $x=2$ and horizontal asymptote $y=3$ shown on sketch.
(A1) Correct intercepts: x-intercept $(-\frac{5}{3}, 0)$ and y-intercept $(0, -\frac{5}{2})$ shown on sketch.
(A2) Correct shape and position of the two branches. Award (A1) for one correct branch.
(b) (M1) Attempt to rearrange $y = \frac{3x+5}{x-2}$ to make $x$ the subject, e.g. $y(x-2) = 3x+5$.

questionBank.modelAnswer:
(a) Asymptotes:
Vertical: $x-2=0 \implies x=2$.
Horizontal: $y = \frac{3}{1} = 3$.
Intercepts:
y-intercept (set $x=0$): $g(0) = \frac{5}{-2} = -2.5$. Point is $(0, -2.5)$.
x-intercept (set $g(x)=0$): $3x+5=0 \implies x = -\frac{5}{3}$. Point is $(-\frac{5}{3}, 0)$.
The sketch should show two hyperbolic branches. Relative to the asymptotes' intersection at $(2,3)$, one branch is in the top-right quadrant. The other branch is in the bottom-left quadrant and passes through the intercepts $(-\frac{5}{3}, 0)$ and $(0, -2.5)$.

(b) Let $y = \frac{3x+5}{x-2}$. To find the inverse, swap $x$ and $y$ and solve for $y$.
$x = \frac{3y+5}{y-2}$
$x(y-2) = 3y+5$
$xy - 2x = 3y+5$
$xy - 3y = 2x+5$
$y(x-3) = 2x+5$
$y = \frac{2x+5}{x-3}$
Therefore, $g^{-1}(x) = \frac{2x+5}{x-3}$.

questionBank.prompt: The diagram shows part of the graph of the function $f(x) = \frac{ax+b}{x+d}$. The graph has a vertical asymptote at $x=3$ and a horizontal asymptote at $y=-2$. The graph passes through the origin $(0,0)$. Find the values of $a$, $b$, and $d$.

markScheme.text: Vertical asymptote at $x=3 \Rightarrow x+d=0$ when $x=3$. So $3+d=0$.
The graph passes through $(0,0)$, so $f(0)=0$. Substituting known values: $\frac{-2(0)+b}{0-3} = 0$.

questionBank.modelAnswer:
The general form of the function is $f(x) = \frac{ax+b}{x+d}$.

The equation of the vertical asymptote is given by setting the denominator to zero, $x+d=0$, so $x=-d$. We are given the vertical asymptote is $x=3$. Therefore, $-d=3$, which means $d=-3$.

The equation of the horizontal asymptote is given by the ratio of the coefficients of $x$, $y=\frac{a}{1}=a$. We are given the horizontal asymptote is $y=-2$. Therefore, $a=-2$.

So far, the function is $f(x) = \frac{-2x+b}{x-3}$.

The graph passes through the origin $(0,0)$, so we can substitute these coordinates into the function's equation: $f(0)=0$.
$0 = \frac{-2(0)+b}{0-3}$
$0 = \frac{b}{-3}$
This implies that $b=0$.

Thus, the values are $a=-2$, $b=0$, and $d=-3$.

questionBank.prompt:
Let $f(x) = \frac{3x-5}{x-1}$.
(a) Find the coordinates of the points where the graph of $f$ intersects the line $y=x-1$. [3]
(b) The graph of a function $g$ is obtained by translating the graph of $f$ by the vector $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$. Find the equation for $g(x)$. [2]
(c) Find the equations of the asymptotes of the graph of $g$. [2]

markScheme.text: Solving the quadratic gives $x=2$ and $x=3$. The points are $(2,1)$ and $(3,2)$.
Asymptotes of $f$ are $x=1, y=3$. Applying translation vector $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$ to the asymptotes.

questionBank.modelAnswer:
(a) To find the points of intersection, we set the two equations equal to each other.
$\frac{3x-5}{x-1} = x-1$
$3x-5 = (x-1)^2$
$3x-5 = x^2 - 2x + 1$
$0 = x^2 - 5x + 6$
Factoring the quadratic gives:
$(x-2)(x-3) = 0$
So, $x=2$ or $x=3$.
For each x-value, we find the corresponding y-value using $y=x-1$.
If $x=2$, $y = 2-1 = 1$. The point is $(2,1)$.
If $x=3$, $y = 3-1 = 2$. The point is $(3,2)$.

(b) A translation by the vector $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$ means we replace $x$ with $(x-1)$ and subtract 3 from the function. So, $g(x) = f(x-1) - 3$.
$g(x) = \frac{3(x-1)-5}{(x-1)-1} - 3$
$g(x) = \frac{3x-3-5}{x-2} - 3$
$g(x) = \frac{3x-8}{x-2} - 3$
To write this as a single fraction:
$g(x) = \frac{3x-8}{x-2} - \frac{3(x-2)}{x-2} = \frac{3x-8 - (3x-6)}{x-2} = \frac{3x-8-3x+6}{x-2} = \frac{-2}{x-2}$.
So, $g(x) = \frac{-2}{x-2}$.

(c) The asymptotes of $f(x) = \frac{3x-5}{x-1}$ are the vertical asymptote $x=1$ and the horizontal asymptote $y=3$.
The translation $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -3 \end{pmatrix}$ shifts the vertical asymptote 1 unit to the right and the horizontal asymptote 3 units down.
New vertical asymptote: $x = 1+1 = 2$.
New horizontal asymptote: $y = 3-3 = 0$.
Alternatively, from the equation $g(x) = \frac{-2}{x-2}$, the vertical asymptote is where the denominator is zero, $x-2=0 \Rightarrow x=2$. The horizontal asymptote is $y=0$ as the degree of the denominator is greater than the degree of the numerator.
